content.problemStatement:
Chef is participating in an ICPC regional contest, in which there is a total of NNN problems (numbered 111 through NNN) with varying difficulties. For each valid iii, the iii-th easiest problem is problem AiA_iAi​.

After a team solves a problem, a balloon with a colour representing that problem is tied next to their desk. Chef is fond of colours in **VIBGYOR**, which are representative of the problems with numbers 111 through 777. The remaining problems have their own representative colours too.

Find the minimum number of problems which Chef's team needs to solve in order to get all the balloons for problems 111 through 777 (and possibly some other balloons too) tied next to their desk, if you know that Chef's team knows the difficulties of all problems and solves the problems in increasing order of difficulty.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (7 ≤ N ≤ 15). The second line contains N space‑separated distinct integers A1, A2, …, AN – a permutation of {1,…,N}. The i‑th integer denotes the problem number of the i‑th easiest problem.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the minimum number of problems Chef's team must solve to have obtained balloons for all problems numbered 1 through 7.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we locate the last occurrence of any number from 1 to 7 in the given order. The positions are 7, 8 and 8 respectively, which are the required minimum numbers of solved problems.
All required problems appear by the 7th position, so Chef must solve all 7 problems.
The numbers 1‑7 appear at positions 4‑10; the latest is position 10.

solution.approach:
Chef solves problems in the order of increasing difficulty, i.e. in the order given by the array A. To have balloons for problems 1…7 we need to solve every problem whose number lies in this set. The moment when the last of these seven problems appears in the solving order determines the answer. Therefore, for each test case we scan the array from left to right, keep the maximum index (1‑based) at which a value ≤ 7 occurs, and output this maximum. This is a single linear pass.

Algorithm:
1. Read T.
2. For each test case:
   a. Read N and the permutation A.
   b. Initialise answer = 0.
   c. For i from 1 to N:
        if A[i] ≤ 7 then answer = i (the loop ensures the last such i is stored).
   d. Print answer.
The algorithm is O(N) per test case and uses O(1) additional memory (apart from the input array).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N ≤ 15, so overall O(T·N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) to store the permutation (can be O(1) if processed on the fly).
